messages.content:
absorb this information, you will need it for my next command:


Week 1: Effective Teaming Part 1

Life cycle of a team includes forming, storming, norming, performing, and adjourning
Forming: Introduction and getting to know each other and identifying team goals
Storming: How to address first team setback and power struggles
Norming: Cooperative actions 
Performing: Peak performance, morale, and productivity
Adjourning: Capturing lessons learned and continuous improvement opportunities
Effective team meetings: No agenda no attenda 
Creating a team charter which is consistently updated and serves as a framework for how the team will operate.
Big takeaway from this lecture: It is important to establish how your team will operate and ensure it runs smoothly. Each team meeting needs an agenda to run smoothly and the team must be focused on productivity for the entire duration of the meeting.

Week 2: Effective Teaming Part 2

Team roles such as leader, organizer, writer, editor, and researcher help keep the team structured.
These roles can be rotated to gain experience in each or can be allocated based on the strengths of each individual on the team
Leadership, receiving/giving feedback, and managing conflict are all an important aspect when working in a team on a large project
Takeaway: Team member roles can create structure in a team and ensure people are working on their strengths and have a set role in the team. Having a set process for resolving conflict also ensures team members are not confused and conflicts can be resolved quickly to avoid setbacks in the team. 

Week 3: Networking

Networking is important as you can meet new people and make useful connections, exchange connections, increase contacts, create opportunities, and discover positive influences and role models. 
Goal of networking: Customers, job offers, mentors, friends, experts, and sponsors
Networking is the exchange of information and ideas among people with a common objective, profession or special interest, usually in an informal social setting. Networking often begins with a single point of common ground. 
The goal of networking is to get an advance, the next step that the person you are networking with will take you to help you achieve your goal.
Examples of an advance: An introduction, sending you a follow up article or piece of information, agreeing to help you with advice or support throughout your networking journey, agreeing to act as a reference for you (level 1 only)
Networking contact levels
Level 1: People who know you well, care about you, and would love to help you achieve your goals.
Test: Would they stop you in the street to tell you hello?
Level 2: Typical sources are introductions from level 1 contacts or meeting as a result of a common interest
A warm personal introduction is best. Say thank you to the level 1 who introduced you after the meeting with the level 2.
Level 3 and 3+: Come from introductions from the previous level. Preparations for these meetings must be even stronger as you have weaker ties to these levels than the previous levels. Again, thank the level 1 who introduced you.
Then meet with the person linked to the target and ultimately the person who is the target or the person who can give you the job.
Cold calling can help skip these steps, people in Canada accept meetings 70% of the time.
Takeaway: Networking is the most important way to reach your professional goals and building networking skills is highly important to be able to progress in your professional career. Practicing for each networking interview is highly important and linked to your success in the interview and your overall networking process.

Week 4: Professional & Personal Development

A work plan is an action plan that helps a team to achieve its goals
It is usually in the form of a table or a Gantt chart and consists of key steps or actions to be taken and:
How long they will take to complete
How they interact with each other (interdependencies)
Identify when key decisions or milestones will occur
Who is responsible for each action
How progress will be tracked and communicated 
Helps everyone on the team get aligned with each step of the project and know what their roles and tasks are as well when they are due
Business etiquette allows you to make a good first impression, show respect, build reputation, create strong relationships, reflect confidence, and be a career enabler. 
Takeaway: Work plans and business etiquette are critical tools in reaching goals. Dimple Dalal, our guest for this lecture, also shared her journey to VP of Investment banking at Bank of America. Shared internships she did and how she leveraged her career.

Week 5: NBTA Introduction & Brainstorming

It is important to be specific with recommendations when discussing how to improve your team. If you do not give specific examples and solutions, it is a bullshit reason.
Key components of a business plan:
Objective: To convince investors to invest in a new business idea that will result in a viable business
Business idea: What product will our business make or what service will it provide?
Customer need: How will we attract customers?
Competitive approach: How will our business be able to compete effectively? 
Resources and financial goals: What resources do we need? How can we make a profit?
Implementation plan: How do we launch and operate our business?
Brainstorming is important as it is a simple technique to get lots of ideas on a particular topic that provides quick results while allowing everyone on your team to participate without it being hierarchical.
Poll Everywhere: Why is making a good first impression important?
Takeaway: Brainstorming is the starting point of any good business idea and allows each team member to contribute to the discussion in order to generate a variety of ideas for a project. A business plan is also the main selling point of the business, without it you will not get customers and investors, and hence you will go out of business.

Week 6: Career Panel

Centre for career design presentation during this lecture 
Zain Khan: The first years of your career are brutal. You’re on your own and have no clue what you’re doing. I wish I had a guide back then so I made my own. I went from 0 to VP in 3 years. Here are 11 principles to accelerate your career now:
Hard work is overrated, consistency is underrated.
It's not about how hard you swing your bad, it's about swinging over and over until you learn to hit it out of the park.
Consistency gets you places that hard work doesn’t.
Optimize for learning over salary and role, a high salary is a byproduct of a valuable role, for which you need a valuable skill set.
Goals are bullshit. You never rise to the level of your goals, you always fall to the level of your habits.
Pick a company in a growing industry to come about opportunities.
Build skills that are rarely found together. Example: Storytelling and data analysis
You gain confidence after doing something hard, not before.
Promote your work shamelessly.
A good manager is more important than a good salary as they will mentor you.
Just because it's not your fault doesn’t mean it's not your responsibility.
Never do anything that hurts your reputation.
Work is the local maximum. Life is the global maximum. There is more to life than work and money.
Takeaway: Career design highlights it is important to plan your career and set your goals. Create a plan for all of first year and first year summer and keep yourself on track with this plan, setting goals to be met along the way. Zain Khan’s advice can also help to elevate a business career and provides valuable insight for those newly in the workforce.
Week 7: How New Businesses Attract Customers

The single most important thing a business needs is customers, as they are the foundation of any new business’ success. No customers means no revenue and no business.
Priorities for customers include convincing people to try your product, repeating business with you, and receiving such excellent service they use word of mouth to promote.
The thing provided by the business needs to satisfy a need, work well (highly functional), and keep on working (have high quality).
New businesses need to promote their product to make customers aware of them.
Setting a fair price is important as it is the value the customer is willing to pay while also allowing the business to make a profit. 
Pricing strategies include:
Cost plus
Value pricing
Contingent pricing (Warranties)
Coffee chats:
What are your goals for the meeting? Are you seeking information or are you making a formal request?
You must research the company, industry, and recent news items of the company the interviewee works for
You must do a google search and a linkedin search on who you are meeting
Let the conversation flow during the coffee chat, going off script is ok but have an idea of what you want to ask.
Towards the end, ask for specific closed ended questions you are looking for such as when to submit an application.
Always say thank you after the chat.
Dorenda McNeil, Senior Vice President of Operations, Brand and Talent at Counsel Public Affairs. Taught the class about business etiquette such as shaking hands and standing up when speaking to someone. No phone glancing and full attention.

Week 8: How New Businesses Compete

When firms compete with each other, consumers get the best possible prices, quantity, and quality of goods and services. 
Competition among companies can spur innovation through the invention of new or better products, or more efficient processes.
Competition can lead companies to event lower cost manufacturing processes, which can increase their profits and help them compete, and they can then pass these savings onto the consumer.
Competition can also help businesses to better identify consumers’ needs and then develop new products or services to meet them.
Five key questions each new business must answer in order to compete successfully:
How strong are existing competitors? (Barriers to entry, industry growth, rivalry)
How hard is it for new competitors to enter your market? (Economies of scale, switching costs, government regulations, monopolies)
How much power do your suppliers have over you? (Supplier concentration, forward integration)
How much power do your customers have over you? (Customer/buyer concentration, substitute goods)
Are there substitutes for your product or service? (Functional similarity, price and performance)
Some businesses would like their competitive strategy to be a moat that separates and protects them from their competitors. 
Rogers, Bell, and Telus: Switching fees to make it expensive to change to another competitor
Your doctor: Has so much information on your medical history you wouldn’t switch
Netflix: Outspends competition in developing new shows 
Perfection competition, monopolies, oligopolies
Airport test: Can you be on a delayed flight wait at the airport with this person? Are they able to talk about business passionately for a minute straight without going off topic? Ultimately the goal is to see how well this person can represent the business in a passionate way.
Make business your hobby and passion!

Week 9: Resources & Financial Performance

Resources needed for a new business
Financial: Financial resources for capital expenditures, working capital, salaries, and operating expenses
Intellectual property: Use of patents, licenses, or copyrights
Equipment, space, and raw materials: Machinery, vehicles, equipment for manufacturing and assembly, real estate and furnishings for offices, operations, and distributions, and raw materials
Human Resources: People resources for all functions and activities of the business
Digital: Hardware, software, licenses, and applications required to operate business
Businesses must develop a financial forecast with what they will need, revenues they expect to generate, and costs. Break even point can also be estimated.
Make assumptions for cost of labour, space, and other costs.
Create a projected income statement to give a better picture. 
Use excel to create graphs for revenue ramp up and cost and profit ramp up.

Week 10: Compelling Written Documents & Powerpoint Slide Decks

LinkedIn is important as it is your personal branding and a presentation of yourself and your professional accomplishments.
Effective charts require a headline, chart title, labelled axes.
Must be clear and easy to understand with clear sources and a call on the audience to pay attention to a key message on the chart.
All data must be accurate, with assumptions reasonable and clearly explained.
Analysis must be high impact: Product description title vs. our product is great title.
Information must be presented well without clutter and must be accurate. It must also be eye-catching and easy to read or else users will quickly lose interest.

Week 11: Effective Presentations

When presenting an analysis and insights, the analysis needs to add value to the facts of the problem or opportunity which you are presenting on.
Each slide should answer the question “So what?”
Bullet points are used to describe the key messages, points, or recommendations.
Characteristics of a high impact presentation:
Accurate data and assumptions
High impact analysis
Clear charts and headlines
Strong preparation
Effective Q & A
When preparing for a Q & A, brainstorm potential questions and answer with teammates and present to someone new and see which questions they present.
If you do not know the answer, put a fence around the issue rather than winging it. Say “I don’t know how to research further and let’s meet to discuss in detail by X date”
When introducing yourself, always greet the interviewer with a big smile and thank them for taking the time to allow you to introduce yourself.




Notes From Speakers

Cindy Rzepecki, CPA - Accounting and Forensic Accounting at EY
Toronto audit group (learn a bit of everything such as retail, construction) 
Forensic accounting at EY - help companies figure out fraud, theft, internal or external. (largest fraud found was 100 million stolen in a constitution company)
Helps companies that are in dispute, does a little bit of law work.

Emma De Tommaso - Law student at U of T and previous work in Finance at Tim Horton's

Ambika Maheshwari, CPA - Accounting, Finance at MLSE and Management Consulting in Mergers and Acquisitions at EY
Went to Coca Cola and did supply chain finance
Now she is working in consulting at EY
Month end, forecasting and budgeting 

Week 8: Michelle Gupta
Graduated from Schulich in 2017
She wanted to go into finance 
She wanted to work in a career with working with people collaboration
Brand management 
You basically manage a brand
Having a budget and you have a target and you have to use the budget given and come up with ways to hit that target

Product
Products are so good that consumers recognize the difference. Superior products raise expectations for performance in the category. 
Packaging
Packaging that attracts consumers, conveys brand equity, helps consumers select the best product for their needs.
Brand communication
Exceptional advertising that makes you think, talk, laugh, cry, smile, act and buy and that drives category and brand growth.
Retail execution 
In-store: with the right store coverage, product forms, sizes, price points, shelving and merchandising.
Online: Consumer and customer value.
All these elements presented in a clear and shoppable way at a compelling price.
Information has been absorbed and is ready for your next command. Please proceed with your request.

Answer the following prompt with a full-fledged essay. be personal and introspective. make sure these key points are adressed clearly

Please reflect on all of the companies you have learned about this term, both in MGMT 1000 and across all of your experiences at Schulich so far:
Which company interests you the most? Why?
What does the company do? What opportunities or challenges do they face that you find interesting?
Would you be interested in working for the company when you graduate? Why or why not?

To answer the prompt aboce, talk about EY I want to be a CPA and work in either forensic accounting or mergers in acquisitions. use the information below to help with this assignment, making reference to man named dilshan. He worked at EY and I met with him during our career netwoekign assignment:

What’s the most standout perk of being a CPA?
In four years, Dilshan designed audit plans across media, entertainment, technology, telecom, and non-profit sectors, highlighting the versatility of a CPA’s skill set.
He highlighted a case where his audit expertise played a crucial role in identifying and correcting misstatements for a large technology client.
In the end, the month-long process underscored the adaptability required in accounting, emphasizing how CPAs, equipped with soft skills, are well-suited for cross-functional roles.

What skills/qualities matter most to EY when selecting interns?
Precision is not just a preference but a necessity in accounting, requiring meticulous attention to detail. 
“Effective decision-making stems from critical thinking. We're interested in professionals who are detail-oriented and have a solid work ethic.” 
Leadership and driving processes are vital in accounting and business. EY sponsored Dilshan to co-author an article with the Neurodiversity Centre of Excellence (NoCE), showcasing the company's initiatives that contribute to broader discussions/problems.

Why are case competitions crucial for aspiring CPAs?
With 4 years as a CPA Ontario Ambassador, Dilshan advocates early engagement in case competitions like ACE, STRIVE, APEX, & THRIVE. 
“Judging ACE Goodman Invitationals, I can say demonstrating creativity and problem-solving is your golden ticket to an internship—recruiters love it!”
 Recruiters value practical experience as much as technical knowledge, and accounting requires strong  team work, communication, and presentation skills.

Can you describe your average workday?
On a normal day, Dilshan usually puts in 40-50 hour weeks. This varies on the time of year.
“From January to April, it's 'busy season,' working 14-18 hours a day. Some even sleep in the office! In normal periods, it's mainly checking emails, meetings, and other tasks.”
As an assurance manager, Dilshan spends most of his day overseeing internal controls and audit teams for project planning, timelines, and resource allocation.

Thoughts Confirmed - Accounting Is The Best Fit For Me
Dilshan affirmed my thoughts on the CPA, exemplifying its flexibility, high starting salary, job security, and long-term growth. In just four years, his inspiring leadership as a team supervisor, neurodiversity advocate, and case competition enthusiast, reflects how the Big 4 culture empowers employees to make positive strides both inside and beyond the workplace.

As someone who similarly considered himself a numbers person, enjoys structured processes, in-depth quantitative analysis, teamwork dynamics, and the flexibility to explore,  I know that accounting is a perfect fit for me. I value both individual and team roles. Thus, joining a Big 4 firm immediately after university would allow me to expand my professional network, gain comprehensive training, and explore various specializations to carve out my own niche.

Title: Finding My Path at EY: Aspirations of a Future CPA at the Nexus of Accounting and Innovation

Throughout my academic journey at Schulich, I've been exposed to a multitude of companies, each with their own unique contributions to the world of business. However, one company has captivated my interest above all: Ernst & Young (EY). This global leader in assurance, tax, transaction, and advisory services stands out not just for its prestige and breadth of services, but also for the alignment of its challenges and opportunities with my personal and professional aspirations.

EY is a firm that thrives on transformation and innovation, conducting audits, assisting in complex mergers, and acquisitions, and navigating the labyrinth of forensic accounting. Its multifaceted nature mirrors the multidimensional skill set that a CPA must possess, which strikes a chord with my own desire for versatility in expertise. The sheer adaptability of a CPA within EY's environment is what draws me toward the company. For instance, an audit professional like Dilshan, whom I met during our career networking assignment, illustrated the dexterity required to design audit plans across distinct sectors, from media to non-profit, and underscored how essential a CPA's adaptability is.

What truly resonates with me is the potential to delve into the realm of forensic accounting or mergers and acquisitions at EY. The prospect of being at the vanguard of identifying financial inconsistencies or guiding momentous business integrations is both thrilling and intellectually stimulating. The challenges that EY faces—remaining cutting-edge in a rapidly evolving digital landscape and navigating the complexities of global financial regulation—present the perfect proving grounds for a driven individual like myself.

During my interaction with Dilshan, I was privy to the tangible impact of a CPA's work. The correction of a misstatement for a large tech client illustrated not only the significant responsibility placed upon EY's professionals but also highlighted the gratifying nature of their exhaustive endeavours. I was enamoured by the critical role EY employees play in shaping their clients' futures and, by extension, the global business ecosystem. 

EY is renowned for its meticulous recruitment process, seeking interns who exhibit precision, critical thinking, and a robust work ethic. Dilshan accentuated how EY values leadership, innovative problem-solving, and the ability to drive processes—qualities that resonate with my own strengths and dedication. His experiences as a judge for case competitions underline how crucial these platforms are for budding CPAs to demonstrate their creative thinking and presentation skills.

The thought of long hours during busy season or the complexity of managing multiple projects simultaneously—tasks that Dilshan described—might deter many, but to me, they elucidate the commitment and passion necessary to thrive at EY. The culture at this Big 4 firm, which encourages positive contributions both within and beyond the workplace's confines, inspires me to envision myself as part of their team.

The impactful encounters with professionals like Dilshan have confirmed my resolve. A career at EY would not only allow me to harness my penchant for numbers and structured analysis but would also challenge me to further develop my teamwork dynamics. The firm's ethos of fostering growth and exploration perfectly matches my goal of diving into the deep end post-university to refine my craft amidst the best minds in the industry.

As I stand on the cusp of my professional life, the choice has never been clearer. EY represents an ideal launchpad, a place where my numerical proficiency can be honed, where my yearning for continuous learning can be satisfied, and where my aspiration to influence the future of accounting can be realized. I am steadfast in my conviction that joining EY as a CPA will set me on a path of exponential personal and professional growth, presenting the incomparable opportunity to become a pivotal part of an organization that is not just keeping pace with the ever-changing business landscape but is actively reshaping it.

good, now rewrite the essay to not flag the AI detectors. below I have provided an essay I wrote myself. rewrite what you produced about netflix above to match the tones, language, and sentence structure of the essay below:


Reflecting on the myriad of companies introduced to us throughout the MGMT 1000 course and my extended journey at Schulich, I found myself particularly intrigued by the business model and ethos of Netflix. This streaming giant, which revolutionized the entertainment industry, has not only sustained relevance but has also demonstrated profound adaptability and competitive acumen in the ever-evolving digital landscape.

Netflix, originally a DVD rental service, transformed into a leading streaming platform and subsequently a content creator, curating an expansive library of original series and films. The company’s opportunities lie in its pioneering data-driven content strategy and international expansion. However, the challenge derives from increasing competition, content acquisition costs, and the need to consistently innovate to retain a diverse and demanding consumer base. These complexities paint an intricate portrait of a company that balances creative endeavors with the pressing need to maintain technological and market leadership—a balance that captivates my intellectual curiosity.

Looking forward to my post-graduation ambitions, Netflix emerges as a compelling employer not merely for its industry prestige but due to the alignment with my own aspirations and the key takeaways from the MGMT 1000 program. The emphasis on continuous learning, which Zain Khan highlighted in his talk, is remarkably analogous to Netflix’s culture that values curiosity and personal development.

Moreover, the company’s challenge in navigating high stake decisions amidst intense competition resonates with the decision-making frameworks and leadership qualities underscored in our workshops. Netflix’s environment promotes not just innovative thinking but demands a proficiency in analytical skills, strategic foresight, and agility—qualities I am keen to refine and employ. As someone attracted to the intersection of technology, business, and creativity, the opportunity to contribute to a company that stands at this crossroads is tantalizing.

Incorporating lessons from our professional development lectures—like business etiquette—and personal branding insights gleaned from crafting compelling LinkedIn profiles, I am better equipped to present myself as a candidate who can embrace and enhance the values Netflix upholds. My experience at Schulich has endowed me with the framework to approach business challenges holistically, and Netflix presents the ideal platform to put these learnings into practice. Consequently, this melding of theory and application fortifies my conviction that joining Netflix post-graduation would be not just an inspiring career move, but a resonance of my educational and professional ethos—a synthesis of ambition and learning.

Pondering over the multitude of firms that have been paraded before us in our MGMT 1000 curriculum and my overall Schulich experience, I've developed a keen fascination with Ernst & Young (EY). This firm isn't just a titan in its field, but it is also a paragon of transformation and inventive spirit within the intricate tapestry of the business world.

EY, with its forte in audits, advisory, and a myriad of financial services, beckons not just because it's a powerhouse known across the globe but because it echoes my own desires for a career teeming with variety and challenge. The very essence of an adaptable, sharp CPA - a role exemplified by professionals like Dilshan, whom I encountered during our career-centric networking activities - aligns with my longing for a role that's as dynamic and multifaceted as the business landscape itself.

The opportunity to dive into the depths of forensic accounting or navigate the turbulent waters of mergers and acquisitions at EY is as daunting as it is enthralling. These arenas, accentuated by the prospect of uncovering intricate financial puzzles or shepherding substantial business unions, offer the kind of intellectual rigor I yearn for. Moreover, EY's dance with the challenges of pioneering in a digitized age and its battle to stay abreast with global financial protocols presents the quintessential backdrop for someone enthusiastic about making impactful business contributions.

When I learned of Dilshan's role in correcting crucial misstatements for a tech behemoth, I didn't just see the meticulous nature of work that EY demands. Instead, I glimpsed the satisfaction that comes from playing a critical part in re-shaping a client's financial narrative—reminiscent of the transformative effect Netflix has had on entertainment. It's the realization that one's work at EY has far-reaching implications for the clients and the larger business world that truly captivates and inspires me.

EY's laser focus on precision, its relentless pursuit of critical thinkers endowed with tireless work ethics—characteristics that Dilshan shed light on—echo my sentiments on the professional qualities I hold in high esteem. The organization's emphasis on creative problem-solving, spearheading processes, and leading initiatives, like the Neurodiversity Centre of Excellence, are the type of challenges I'm eager to embrace.

The discussion of the rigorous work hours, particularly during peak seasons, or the intensive management of multiple projects might be intimidating for some. Yet, they highlight the profound commitment and fiery passion required to excel at EY. The firm's ethos, which champions personal growth and societal contributions, aligns with the values ingrained in me by the exceptional education at Schulich.

The consistent engagements with astute professionals like Dilshan have only solidified my conviction that EY is where I envision starting my professional journey. The role of a CPA at such a venerable firm offers the perfect amalgamation of number-centric finesse, collaborative synergy, and the chance to explore various niches. The cultural cornerstones of EY—empowerment, growth, and the encouragement to extend one's impact beyond the office—harmonize with my personal and professional aspirations.

Conclusively, I'm deeply drawn to EY as a springboard for my aspirations. Here, I see myself sharpening my analytical acumen amidst the brightest, stretching my collaborative capacities, and becoming an invaluable player in a firm that doesn't just respond to change—it propels it. My journey at Schulich has prepared me for this leap, and I regard EY as the place where my educational endeavors and vocational pursuits converge in a symphony of growth and opportunity.

Throughout the comprehensive MGMT 1000 course and the entirety of my academic explorations at Schulich, no organization has caught my fancy quite like Ernst & Young (EY). This beacon in the professional services sector illuminates the very qualities I esteem – innovation, integrity, and intelligence. It represents not just a workplace but a crucible of potential where the numerical acumen, ethical grounding, and inventive thought I value are not only sought after but also nurtured.

EY transcends the traditional confines of auditing and accounting, embracing a holistic approach to professional services that include tax, advisory, and an impressive array of strategic business services. From my perspective, what sets EY apart is its pioneering spirit in embracing areas like forensic accounting—a field where each investigation unearths narratives woven into the financial fabric of enterprises. The thrilling opportunity to solve complex puzzles and combat financial crimes, or immerse myself in the intricacies of mergers and acquisitions—guiding businesses through transformative changes—are endeavors I find profoundly compelling.

The company continually strides through the evolving challenges of a tech-centric era, deftly wielding innovation to stay at the vanguard of its industry. Yet, alongside these opportunities for revolutionary growth, EY grapples with formidable challenges like navigating the shifting sands of international financial regulations, or integrating artificial intelligence and data analytics into traditional accounting practices to lead its clients into a new business paradigm.

My admiration for EY is not just rooted in its formidable portfolio or its dynamic workplace environment—it's in the myriad facets of opportunity and challenge that it presents. Dilshan, an inspiring EY professional I had the privilege to network with, spoke of a workday that oscillates with the rhythms of "busy seasons" and project-driven ebbs. He laid bare the landscape of EY's operations, where meticulous effort in auditing could be the bulwark against errant financial declarations, and where attention to detail could steer mammoth corporate mergers towards success or avert the peril of ill-managed acquisitions.

After completing my degree, my ambition is to step through EY's doors as a fresh CPA, eager to carve out a niche within their audit or advisory departments. The allure of being part of a firm that is instrumental in shaping global business practices; a firm that beckons with a promise of ceaseless learning and growth—these are the coordinates of the future I yearn for. With a resume sharpened by the rigorous academic training and diverse extracurricular exposure gained at Schulich, I am primed to not only step into EY's ecosystem but to thrive within it.

Imagine a professional journey where each step is as enlightening as it is challenging—a journey underpinned by the thrill of wrestling with complex fiscal paradigms and emerging victorious. That is the journey I foresee at EY, and it's a journey I am wholeheartedly prepared to embark on.

Looking back on the firms that have been paraded before us in MGMT 1000, I’ve developed a keen fascination with Ernst & Young (EY). This firm isn’t just a titan in its field, but it is also a paragon of transformation and inventive spirit within the business world. It offers the unique blend of industry-leading practices and continuous learning, set in a professional environment that thrives on innovation and critical problem solving. The prospect of joining EY's ranks and contributing to their culture of excellence is not only inspiring but perfectly aligns with my educational journey at Schulich and my professional goals.

infuse personal examples that a student could expeience to back up the claims of this. keep it realivley short: EY, with its forte in audits, advisory, and financial consulting, echoes my own desires for a career teeming with variety and challenge. From my perspective, what sets EY apart from other Big 4 firms is how it offers a wide range of professional services beyond accounting. The Sustainable Investment Challenge is a mock exercise, often found in a business school curriculum, where students are tasked with creating investment portfolios that balance profitability with environmental, social, and governance (ESG) considerations. At Schulich, you would have learned about it through a course or a specific event organized by the school, designed to simulate real-world scenarios where sustainable and ethical business practices are central to financial decision-making. This challenge provides an insightful platform for future accountants to understand the importance of integrating sustainability into their profession.
